reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Right to equal access to quality education; Roma and disadvantaged pupils' documented exclusion from selective tracks despite ability; the public good is realized only when access rules are transparent and SES-blind in operation, not intent alone.
reactions.interest: Disadvantaged and Roma pupils, especially those with demonstrated ability but no family test-prep resources or information-navigation capacity; families for whom selective-track admission is currently gatekept by fee-paying coaching infrastructure.
reactions.public_good_frame: Selection mechanisms should not reproduce poverty and ethnic segregation across generations. A reformed admission rule that is demonstrably less coachable and SES-aware serves the public good by opening access to quality teaching and peer composition for children currently locked out.
reactions.argument: S1 addresses the right problem—test-prep coaching arms races exclude able Roma and low-SES pupils not because they lack ability but because their families cannot afford the £500–£2000/year coaching fees documented in Hungarian education-access literature. Replacing coachability with an SES-aware quota mechanism is the only scenario that explicitly targets the barrier-by-design element. However, the scenario's own evidence status admits the SES indicator does not exist yet (high-risk assumption), and non-state maintainers' voluntary alignment is unenforceable. If only state-maintained schools reform while church/foundation schools absorb the demand of rejected high-SES applicants (offsetting), the net equity gain may be marginal. The condition that wins me over is: a pilot-validated, publicly transparent SES proxy that cannot be gamed (e.g., self-reported data is vulnerable to misrepresentation), coupled with enforceable quotas across all maintainer types—not negotiated alignment. Without that, S1 risks becoming a theatrical reform that preserves informal selection through interviews and discretionary criteria while claiming to have solved the problem.
reactions.condition_to_change: A costed, multi-year pilot demonstrating: (1) an SES proxy that is validated, transparent, and resistant to gaming; (2) binding (not voluntary) quota compliance across church/foundation and state-maintained schools, monitored; (3) elimination of discretionary admission criteria (interviews, reputation) that re-enable informal SES sorting; (4) evidence that admitted low-SES pupils are not tracked into lower-quality streams within selective schools post-admission. Absent these, the reform remains a quota on paper.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Right to equal access to quality education; opposition to mechanisms that shrink the resource base for disadvantaged pupils; Roma and low-SES pupils' documented concentration in general-track schools, meaning any cap that tightens selection without structural reform deepens segregation.
reactions.interest: Disadvantaged and Roma pupils currently in general-track schools (the vast majority); families already excluded from selective tracks, whose peer composition and resource conditions will worsen if caps shrink and non-state schools expand selective enrollment.
reactions.public_good_frame: Phasing down selective seats without abolishing selection does not reduce inequality—it concentrates it. Capped but still-selective state schools become even more SES-skewed (fewer seats, same applicant skew); non-state offsetting relocates sorting outside democratic oversight. The public good requires either structural abolition of early selection or radical admission reform with enforceable equity safeguards, not managed scarcity.
reactions.argument: S2 is a harm-by-attrition strategy dressed as gradual reform. The scenario's own analysis admits: (1) residual selective places become more SES-skewed mechanically (fewer seats, same or worsening applicant pool) because admission rules do not change; (2) non-state maintainers are 'near-certain' to expand to absorb displaced high-SES demand, meaning the overall sorting does not reduce, just migrate. For Roma and low-SES pupils in general-track schools, this is net-negative: peer composition in general schools worsens as the top performers stay longer only if high-SES families do not exit to non-state schools—but the scenario's own political-risk section flags exactly that exit as likely. The decade-long implementation window is also a liability: across two electoral cycles with no documented precedent of political durability in Hungary, commitment will erode, leaving the cap in place but its equity logic abandoned. Capping without reforming admission or abolishing selection is a stealth legitimization of early selection as permanent policy—it just shrinks the queue rather than asking why the queue exists.
reactions.condition_to_change: S2 becomes acceptable only if coupled with: (1) concurrent S1-type binding admission reform (SES quotas, non-coachable assessment, enforceable across all maintainers) that prevents residual seats from becoming more SES-skewed; (2) enforceable non-state enrollment caps or transparency rules that prevent offsetting; (3) proof that political commitment survives a full electoral cycle before caps begin. Without those, oppose.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Right to equal access to quality education and mixed-ability peer groups; Roma and disadvantaged pupils' documented harm from early selection and segregation; the public good is realized only when sorting mechanisms that reproduce poverty are abolished, not reformed or compensated around.
reactions.interest: Roma and disadvantaged pupils, especially those with ability but no test-prep access or information navigation—the population currently excluded from selective tracks and concentrated in under-resourced general schools. Also Roma and disadvantaged pupils in rural areas whose local schools are drained of high performers by urban selective intake, collapsing rural educational access.
reactions.public_good_frame: Early selection at age 10-12 is a sorting mechanism that, combined with SES-skewed coaching and maintainer plurality, produces segregation and inequality by design. Abolishing it removes the mechanism itself, not merely tweaking its margins. The public good requires all pupils to share common schools through age 14, building mixed-ability peer groups and preventing institutional stratification from calcifying in childhood.
reactions.argument: S3 is the only scenario that addresses the structural root—not the symptoms. The cross-national evidence (Hanushek & Woessmann 2006) is strong: earlier tracking increases outcome inequality without raising mean performance. Reversing that (delayed tracking to age 14) should reduce inequality without a mean-cost. Yes, the Hungarian peer-effect mechanism is contested/weak, but that weaknesses in evidence-confidence, not evidence of harm. The real risk is not that S3 will fail to reduce inequality, but that choice-architecture and non-state expansion will relocate sorting rather than remove it—but that is a failure of implementation safeguards, not the structural reform itself. The scenario's own implementation steps include pilot monitoring and choice-architecture safeguards; if those are done rigorously, the risk is addressable. For Roma and disadvantaged pupils, the alternative scenarios all leave early selection in place: S1 tries to reweight it (but rests on an SES indicator that does not exist), S2 shrinks it (but likely offsets it into non-state expansion), S4 compensates around it (but never removes the mechanism that causes stratification). Only S3 removes the mechanism. The cost is real (teacher retraining, facility re-adaptation), but it is a one-time investment in structural equity, not a permanent subsidy to a flawed system. Political durability is a real risk; that is why the implementation includes a Go/No-Go review at year 4 before full rollout, and why cross-party commitment is essential. But the solution is not to avoid structural reform—it is to build political durability through transparent process and pilot evidence.
reactions.condition_to_change: Support is conditional on: (1) pilot rollout (year 2-4) in a representative set of districts, with rigorous monitoring of inter-school choice and non-state enrollment shifts, and published Go/No-Go decision criteria at year 4; (2) enforceable choice-architecture safeguards (e.g., bans on selective non-state enrollment post-abolition, or transparent enrollment rules preventing cream-skimming) implemented concurrent with rollout, not as afterthought; (3) documented teacher retraining delivery capacity assessed before year-1 legislation; (4) binding cross-party pact on continuity, with explicit consequences for reversal (e.g., reversal triggers an independent equity audit). Lose support if: implementation skips pilots or choice-safeguards, or if legislative reversal is threatened within the first 8 years (two electoral cycles).

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Opposition to mechanisms that compensate for structural inequality rather than abolishing it; Roma and disadvantaged pupils' documented harm from early selection itself, not merely from resource scarcity; the public good requires removing sorting mechanisms, not indefinitely funding their downstream consequences.
reactions.interest: Roma and disadvantaged pupils, especially those with ability but excluded from selective tracks; families for whom 'compensation' in under-resourced general schools is a permanent, not temporary, status—meaning compensation becomes institutionalized as 'good enough for poor kids' rather than a bridge to equality.
reactions.public_good_frame: Compensation without structural reform is a form of managed inequality. It tells Roma and low-SES families: 'Your children will be sorted into lower-status schools at age 10, but we will fund that lower-status tier adequately so you stop complaining.' That is not the public good—it is permanent stratification with a human-services budget line. The public good requires abolishing the sorting mechanism itself so that all children have access to quality, mixed-ability schools as a right, not as a compensated consolation prize.
reactions.argument: S4 is the option that most directly serves administrative continuity and political ease, not equity. The scenario's own evidence status admits: (1) the Portuguese precedent did not involve early selection at Hungary's age (10-12), so transferability is weak; (2) no country has demonstrated that compensation can offset (rather than merely mask) the effects of early sorting at Hungary's selection age; (3) the scenario's own political-risks section flags that compensation may 'entrench rather than dismantle the sorting engine' by removing political pressure for structural reform. That is the core problem: S4 makes early selection permanent and acceptable by wrapping it in targeted funding. For Roma and disadvantaged pupils, this is a historic trap. Compensation programs are politically fragile—the scenario itself notes targeted funding is 'historically the first line item cut during fiscal consolidation.' If TEIP-style funding is cut (likely), then Roma and disadvantaged pupils are left with permanent sorting and no compensation. Meanwhile, S3 eliminates the sorting mechanism itself, which is a permanent change that cannot be easily reversed without politically visible structural action. From a Roma-rights perspective, a structural reform that sticks is better than a compensation program that is vulnerable every budget cycle. The scenario also admits teacher shortages will limit remedial-support quality—which means Hungary's already-constrained teacher supply will be diverted to 'fixing' general-school deficits rather than removed from selection itself.
reactions.condition_to_change: S4 becomes acceptable only if: (1) it is an explicitly temporary bridge to S3, with a legislated timeline (e.g., 'TEIP-style funding for 5 years, followed by structural abolition of selective entry'); (2) TEIP funding is constitutionally ring-fenced against fiscal consolidation cuts; (3) concurrent with funding, admissions rules in selective schools are reformed per S1 (SES quotas, non-coachable assessment, binding across all maintainers) to prevent residual selection from remaining SES-skewed. Without those, oppose—because S4 as written entrenches early selection as permanent policy with a budget that is politically fragile.
